content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 4, 1990


James E. Erickson
Nossaman, Gunther, Knox & Elliott
Center Tower
650 Town Center Drive, Suite 1250
Costa Mesa, CA 92626-9000

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-90-537

Dear Mr. Erickson:

We have received your letter dated August 9, 1990, seeking written confirmation of telephone advice provided to you on that date by this agency regarding your responsibilities under the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act") on behalf of the clients represented by your firm. We are unable to provide you with the confirmation you seek.

Your firm represents various consortia that the California Department of Transportation (CalTrans) has qualified to submit proposals for privatization demonstration projects (toll-roads, toll bridges, etc.) under the AB 680 transportation privatization program.

Prior to making decisions on specific projects or programs, CalTrans invited the consortia to participate in conferences that would draft common, "boilerplate" provisions (such as franchise, development, operations and maintenance agreements) to be included in any subsequent contract between CalTrans and the consortia for a particular demonstration project or projects in the future. These conferences, in other words, are not for the purpose of selecting - and awarding the contract for - the actual demonstration projects, but instead are designed to achieve consensus on portions of the language to be included in the contract for the project ultimately selected.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-90-537
Page 2

     Your conclusion, originally confirmed by telephone, was that
the consortia's participation in this process did not constitute
an attempt to influence administrative action pursuant to Section
82032, and thereby did not require your firm to register on their
behalf. It was your conclusion that the consortia's participation in the process by which agreement was obtained on "boilerplate" for a future and non-specified contract does not constitute
"administrative action" as defined in Section 82002.

     However, the consortia's participation in such a process does
appear to constitute a "quasi-legislative proceeding" for purposes
of Section 82002. The Commission has previously advised that a
relevant consideration in determining whether a proceeding was
quasi-legislative was whether the action taken involved "an orient
tation towards future events," or "rules and regulations which
establish standards for future conduct" to which a private party
must conform in the future. (In re Leonard 2 FPPC Ops. 54 (1976),
copy enclosed.)

     Here, the result of the consortia's participation in the process whereby boilerplate language will be adopted for future contracts will be contractual language that establishes "standards
for future conduct." In this instance, the conduct of the successful bidders on the subsequent contracts awarded by CalTrans under
the transportation privatization program. As such, it appears
that the decisions on appropriate contractual boilerplate constitute the proposal, drafting, consideration and enactment of "other
action" in a quasi-legislative proceeding for purposes of Section
82002. As the participants in this process are your clients, you
would therefore be required to register under Section 82032.

________________

     It was also your conclusion that the consortia's participation
in this process exempted it from any registration requirements
under Regulation 18202(a)(7). This section excludes a proceeding
at which a grant or contract is awarded from the definition of
"quasi-legislative proceeding" under Section 82002. However,
since you have described the process in which the consortia will
participate as one which involves making decisions on language for
common provisions in any future contract between CalTrans and the
consortia, these conferences are not for the purpose of selecting
the actual demonstration projects or awarding the appropriate
contracts for such projects.

     Section 82002 defines administrative action as the proposal,
drafting, consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by any
state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding.

File No. A-90-537
Page 3

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

by: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:plh

Enclosure
